content.text: A reference architecture is a generic, reusable pattern — the RAG pattern, the planner-executor pattern, the HITL routing pattern. A solution blueprint instantiates one or more reference architectures for a specific enterprise context, binding them to technology choices, organisational constraints, compliance requirements, and operational standards that are already in place.

The distinction matters in enterprise delivery: a reference architecture tells you how a class of system should be designed; a blueprint tells a project team exactly what they need to build this particular system within this particular organisation. A blueprint should be detailed enough that a project team can use it to populate a delivery backlog without making major architectural decisions themselves.

A blueprint that leaves technology choices open is not a blueprint — it is a reference architecture with extra pages. Blueprints bind choices. If the binding is wrong, update the blueprint, not the project.

attrs.alt: GenAI Solution Blueprint anatomy — nine standard components across three rows: system context, component diagram, data flows, security model; NFR targets, compliance checklist, ADR references; deployment model, observability plan, runbook stub, evaluation framework

content.text: Every GenAI solution blueprint in the enterprise catalog contains the same nine components. This consistency allows DRB reviewers, security teams, and product owners to navigate any blueprint without relearning a new structure.

The system context diagram shows the system being built, the human actors who interact with it, and the external systems it depends on or integrates with. Trust boundaries are drawn explicitly — which actors are internal, which are third parties, and which are regulated entities.

The system context also states what is explicitly out of scope: systems the project will not integrate with in this phase, populations of users not covered, data types deliberately excluded. Out-of-scope statements prevent scope creep during delivery.
The component diagram decomposes the system boundary into named services, models, data stores, queues, and APIs. For each component, the diagram notes:
The technology choice (e.g., "Vector Store: pgvector on Aurora PostgreSQL 15")
Whether the component is new (built for this project), existing (reused from another system), or shared (used by multiple systems simultaneously)
Data flows document how data moves through the system, with classification labels on each flow:
The security model defines how every actor and service authenticates and what it is authorised to do. For GenAI systems, this includes:
OIDC provider, token lifetime, MFA requirement
Which roles can query, which can view retrieved context, which can access audit logs
Workload identity or mTLS for internal calls; API keys or OAuth client credentials for external APIs
Which secrets are stored in Vault / AWS Secrets Manager; rotation policy; no secrets in environment variables or configuration files
VPC peering, private endpoints for cloud services, egress filtering
What events are logged (every LLM call, every vector store query, every user interaction), where logs are sent, and who can read them
Non-functional requirements for GenAI systems have GenAI-specific dimensions beyond standard latency and availability targets:
APM span from gateway to response
Load test at P95 latency target
External health check; circuit breaker trips at 3 consecutive failures
Token counter middleware; daily cost alert
Max 6 000 tokens for context; 1 000 for system prompt

The compliance checklist maps the system to applicable regulatory obligations. It is signed off by the DPO (for data protection obligations) and Legal (for sector-specific obligations) before the production readiness gate:

compliance_checklist:
  gdpr:
    - item: Data Protection Impact Assessment completed
      status: done
      evidence: DPIA-2026-047.pdf
    - item: Data Processing Agreement with LLM provider signed
      status: done
      evidence: DPA-OpenAI-2026-01.pdf
    - item: Data subject access request workflow documented
      status: in_progress
      owner: dpo@company.com
      due: 2026-08-01
    - item: Right to erasure procedure covers vector store chunks
      status: done
      evidence: runbook/data-erasure.md

  ai_act:
    - item: Risk classification documented (Limited Risk — AI chatbot with disclosure)
      status: done
    - item: AI system disclosure to users implemented
      status: done
      evidence: UI screenshot showing disclosure banner

  sector_specific:
    - item: FCA COBS 2.1 — no regulated advice; disclaimer in every response
      status: done
    - item: ICO guidance on AI in financial services — reviewed and gaps addressed
      status: done
      evidence: gap-analysis-2026-06.pdf

Every architectural decision that shaped the blueprint is referenced here, along with the decision status. If an ADR is superseded or deprecated after the blueprint is published, the reference in the blueprint is flagged for review:
Constrains LLM choice to OpenAI GPT-4o or Anthropic Claude 3
Mandates pgvector for new projects unless approved exception filed
Requires input sanitisation and output validation layers
Every LLM call logged with prompt hash, token counts, response hash
Exception to ADR-007 for embedding path; conditions tracked in waiver register
The catalog is a version-controlled repository of approved blueprints. Each entry contains the nine components above plus a catalog header that enables discovery:

# catalog header — stored in blueprints/<id>/blueprint.yaml
blueprint:
  id: bp-genai-rag-insurance-fnol
  name: RAG-Based FNOL Claims Assistant
  version: "1.2"
  status: approved          # draft | approved | deprecated
  approved_by: drb@company.com
  approved_date: 2026-06-15
  reference_architectures:
    - rag-reference-architecture
    - human-in-the-loop-architecture
  tags:
    - insurance
    - claims
    - rag
    - customer-facing
  use_case_fit:
    - "Customer or handler queries against structured + unstructured claims knowledge"
    - "Policy clause retrieval and interpretation"
    - "NOT suitable for: binding coverage decisions without human review"
  constraints:
    - "LLM provider must be on approved list (ADR-007)"
    - "Customer PII requires DPA before processing (ADR-015)"
    - "Human-in-the-loop gate mandatory for coverage-related outputs"
  known_projects:
    - FNOL-GenAI (live, 2026-Q2)
    - Claims-Assist-Pilot (PoC, 2026-Q3)
  owner: principal-architect@company.com
  next_review: 2027-01-01

The catalog header enables project teams to search for blueprints by tag, use case, or reference architecture. It also tracks which live projects are using each blueprint — a critical link for impact assessment when an ADR changes or a security vulnerability is found in a shared component.
Blueprints evolve as technology, regulation, and lessons from live projects accumulate. The versioning scheme captures:
Clarification, documentation fix, minor component update
New component added, NFR target changed, new compliance requirement
Yes — DRB sign-off on the update
Breaking change — different reference architecture, new data classification, security model overhaul
Yes — full DRB review treating it as a new blueprint
Replaced by a better blueprint; known vulnerabilities; tech stack end-of-life
Yes — DRB records replacement and migration guidance

Projects that are already using a blueprint when it reaches a minor or major version are notified and given a migration window. The blueprint catalog tracks the "minimum supported version" for production deployments — projects on deprecated versions must migrate or file a waiver.

When a project selects a blueprint, they instantiate it: filling in the project-specific values for each placeholder in the nine components. The instantiation produces a project solution document — a blueprint with all blanks filled in, which becomes the project's architectural artefact reviewed at the DRB gate.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class BlueprintInstance:
    blueprint_id: str
    blueprint_version: str
    project_id: str
    project_name: str

    # Component 1 — System Context
    primary_actors: list[str]
    external_systems: list[str]
    trust_boundary_notes: str
    out_of_scope: list[str]

    # Component 3 — Data Flows (project-specific overrides)
    data_classification_overrides: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)

    # Component 4 — Security
    oidc_provider: str = ""
    rbac_roles: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    secret_store: str = ""

    # Component 5 — NFR overrides (project may tighten defaults)
    latency_p95_ms: int = 3000
    cost_per_request_gbp: float = 0.02

    # Component 6 — Compliance
    dpia_reference: Optional[str] = None
    dpa_references: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    # Component 7 — ADR additions (project-specific ADRs on top of blueprint ADRs)
    additional_adrs: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        errors = []
        if not self.dpia_reference:
            errors.append("DPIA reference is required before DRB submission")
        if not self.dpa_references:
            errors.append("At least one DPA reference is required for LLM/embedding API use")
        if not self.secret_store:
            errors.append("Secret store must be specified — cannot use environment variables")
        return errors

# Example: FNOL project instantiation
fnol_instance = BlueprintInstance(
    blueprint_id="bp-genai-rag-insurance-fnol",
    blueprint_version="1.2",
    project_id="FNOL-GenAI",
    project_name="FNOL Claims GenAI Assistant",
    primary_actors=["Claims Handler", "Customer (self-service portal)"],
    external_systems=["Core Claims System (REST)", "Policy DB (read replica)", "Auth: Azure AD B2C"],
    trust_boundary_notes="Customer portal operates in DMZ; claims handler on internal network only",
    out_of_scope=["Commercial lines claims", "Litigation support", "Actuarial reporting"],
    oidc_provider="Azure AD B2C",
    rbac_roles=["ClaimsHandler", "ClaimsManager", "AuditViewer"],
    secret_store="Azure Key Vault (project: fnol-genai-kv)",
    dpia_reference="DPIA-2026-047",
    dpa_references=["DPA-OpenAI-2026-01", "DPA-Pinecone-2026-03"],
    additional_adrs=["ADR-FNOL-001: FNOL Classification Model", "ADR-FNOL-002: Policy Chunk Hierarchy"],
)

errors = fnol_instance.validate()
if errors:
    for e in errors:
        print(f"VALIDATION ERROR: {e}")
else:
    print("Blueprint instance valid — ready for DRB submission")
The blueprint catalog requires ongoing governance to remain useful:
Version status, project usage count, pending compliance updates
Email to project tech leads; migration window set
Blueprints marked deprecated; replacement guidance published
Verify all production projects are on supported blueprint versions

A blueprint catalog that nobody trusts is worse than no catalog — teams will build ad hoc solutions and call them blueprints. Trust requires that blueprints are kept current, that they reflect real delivery experience, and that the governance overhead of adopting one is lower than the overhead of designing from scratch.
Project architects select a blueprint by matching three criteria:
Which reference architecture(s) does the project need? Cross-reference the blueprint catalog's reference_architectures field.
Does the blueprint's use_case_fit description match the project's functional requirements? Use case mismatches are harder to fix than technology mismatches.
Can the project meet the blueprint's constraints? If a constraint requires a DPA that the project cannot obtain in time, the project needs an exception process, not a different blueprint.

When no existing blueprint fits, the project team proposes a new blueprint. The proposal goes through the DRB, and if approved, the new blueprint is added to the catalog so future projects benefit from the work. This ensures that architectural novelty accumulates as institutional knowledge rather than being reinvented in each project's documentation.

A solution blueprint is a project-ready instantiation of one or more reference architectures, binding technology choices and organisational constraints into a single artefact that a delivery team can act from. The nine standard components — system context, component diagram, data flows, security model, NFR targets, compliance checklist, ADR references, deployment model, and observability/evaluation frameworks — give the DRB a complete picture at each governance gate.
Blueprints bind; reference architectures suggest. If an enterprise blueprint leaves technology choices open, it is not a blueprint.
The catalog header (id, version, tags, use case fit, constraints, known projects) enables discovery and impact assessment.
Versioning with DRB-gated minor/major changes keeps blueprints current without allowing uncontrolled drift.
Blueprint instantiation produces the project solution document — the artefact reviewed at DRB gates.
Every production project must be traceable to an approved, non-deprecated blueprint version.
